sourceFiles.name: thermal-conflict

results.name: 05_conflicting_requirements
results.rawText:
Model characterisation:
- System purpose: A thermal protection/control fragment for a system that monitors temperature and triggers emergency shutdown and cooling fan activation.
- Operational domain: Generic thermal management / industrial or embedded control domain.
- Model scope: Small model fragment containing two requirement definitions and one cooling-related part definition with threshold attributes.
- Known scope limitations: No behavior model, no interfaces, no allocations, no protocol definitions, no requirement-to-part traceability (`satisfy`/`refine`) beyond co-presence in the package. Checks requiring state machines, activities, interfaces, processing capacity, environmental zones, or full requirement hierarchy are only assessable insofar as explicit text is present.

ISSUE [CONFLICT-001]
Element(s):     thermal_system::EmergencyShutdown; thermal_system::CoolingFanActivation; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::emergencyShutdownThresholdC; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::coolingFanStartThresholdC
Check:          1. Numeric Constraint Conflicts
Finding:        The model defines emergency shutdown at 80°C while fan activation is defined later at 85°C in the requirement text and 86°C in the part attributes. This creates a hard conflict in the intended thermal response sequence: once the system shuts down at 80°C, the cooling fan activation threshold at 85/86°C cannot be reached in normal operation, so the fan activation condition is incompatible with the shutdown condition.
Evidence:       Requirement text: "The system shall enter emergency shutdown when temperature reaches 80 degrees Celsius." Requirement text: "The cooling fan shall turn on when temperature reaches 85 degrees Celsius." Part attributes: "attribute emergencyShutdownThresholdC : Real = 80.0;" and "attribute coolingFanStartThresholdC : Real = 86.0;"
Conflict Type:  Hard
Score:          0.1  -  Critical defect because the two thermal thresholds define an unachievable control regime in which shutdown occurs before the cooling action is commanded.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Thermal control design cannot be implemented consistently; verification of overtemperature response will produce contradictory expected behavior, and design decisions about whether cooling or shutdown is the primary protective action are blocked.
Recommendation: Lower the cooling-fan activation threshold below the shutdown threshold, or redefine the shutdown condition to occur after failed cooling escalation; then add explicit behavioral sequencing and traceability showing how the fan and shutdown thresholds are intended to interact.

ISSUE [CONFLICT-002]
Element(s):     thermal_system::CoolingFanActivation; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::coolingFanStartThresholdC
Check:          4. Requirement-Level Conflicts
Finding:        The requirement says the cooling fan shall turn on at 85°C, but the model attribute sets the fan start threshold to 86.0°C. These two specifications impose contradictory trigger values for the same function.
Evidence:       Requirement text: "The cooling fan shall turn on when temperature reaches 85 degrees Celsius." Part attribute: "attribute coolingFanStartThresholdC : Real = 86.0;"
Conflict Type:  Hard
Score:          0.2  -  Significant defect because implementation and verification could use different trigger values, producing wrong pass/fail results and inconsistent design behavior.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Requirement verification is ambiguous: a test at 85°C would pass against the requirement but fail against the implemented threshold value. This undermines design validation and acceptance testing.
Recommendation: Align the requirement and model value to a single threshold, and add a `satisfy` relationship or rationale tying the implemented attribute to the requirement.

ISSUE [CONFLICT-003]
Element(s):     thermal_system::EmergencyShutdown; thermal_system::CoolingFanActivation
Check:          6. Safety vs. Functional Conflicts
Finding:        The safety-oriented emergency shutdown requirement conflicts with the functional cooling requirement because shutdown is mandated at a lower temperature than the fan activation point. The safety action precludes execution of the functional mitigation action, with no priority/resolution logic describing whether cooling should occur before shutdown or during shutdown.
Evidence:       "The system shall enter emergency shutdown when temperature reaches 80 degrees Celsius." contrasted with "The cooling fan shall turn on when temperature reaches 85 degrees Celsius."
Conflict Type:  Hard
Score:          0.1  -  Critical defect because the safety requirement disables or supersedes the functional mitigation before that mitigation can ever occur.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Safety analysis and functional design will reach incompatible conclusions about intended overtemperature handling, preventing a coherent fault response strategy from being baselined.
Recommendation: Define explicit precedence and mode logic: e.g., fan activates at a lower threshold, shutdown occurs only if temperature continues to rise, or document that fan operation is unavailable/irrelevant once shutdown is entered.

ISSUE [CONFLICT-004]
Element(s):     thermal_system::EmergencyShutdown; thermal_system::CoolingFanActivation; thermal_system::CoolingSystem
Check:          7. Priority & Resolution
Finding:        The model contains an apparent conflict between shutdown and cooling thresholds, but no documented priority ordering, rationale, `satisfy`, or `refine` relationship explains how the conflict is resolved in the design.
Evidence:       The package contains requirement definitions and a part definition with thresholds, but no explicit priority statement, no rationale note, and no `satisfy`/`refine` relations resolving the threshold interaction.
Conflict Type:  Latent
Score:          0.3  -  Significant defect because conflicting requirements are present without any modeled resolution mechanism, forcing downstream engineers to guess intent.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Stakeholders cannot determine which requirement governs implementation or verification, increasing the chance of inconsistent subsystem behavior and disputed acceptance criteria.
Recommendation: Add explicit requirement relationships and rationale identifying precedence, intended operational sequence, and how attribute values satisfy the approved requirement set.

CHECK PASSED: 2. Behavioural Conflicts  -  No state machine, activity, transition, trigger, guard, or execution-timing behavior is present, so behavioral conflict checks are not applicable to this fragment beyond the threshold conflicts already captured elsewhere.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Interface & Protocol Conflicts  -  No ports, flows, connections, interfaces, or protocol specifications are present in the model fragment, so no interface/protocol conflict could be examined.

CHECK PASSED: 5. Allocation Conflicts  -  No functional allocations, processors, hardware/software mappings, or location constraints are modeled, so allocation conflict checks are not applicable.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| CONFLICT-001 | 0.1 | HIGH | Shutdown threshold at 80°C conflicts with fan activation at 85/86°C, making the cooling response unreachable. |
| CONFLICT-002 | 0.2 | HIGH | Fan activation requirement specifies 85°C while the modeled attribute specifies 86°C. |
| CONFLICT-003 | 0.1 | HIGH | Safety shutdown occurs before the functional cooling action can execute. |
| CONFLICT-004 | 0.3 | HIGH | No documented priority or resolution explains the conflicting shutdown and cooling requirements. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.18

results.name: 04_redundancy
results.rawText:
Model Characterisation
- System purpose: A thermal protection/control model for a system that monitors temperature and triggers emergency shutdown and cooling fan behaviour.
- Operational domain: General engineered thermal control / industrial or embedded control context.
- Model scope: Small model fragment containing two requirements and two structural definitions.
- Known scope limitations: No behaviour model, no state machines, no verification cases, no interfaces, no allocations, no packages beyond one package fragment. Checks requiring behavioural activation logic, relationship multiplicity, or decomposition depth are only assessable to a limited extent from the provided text.

CHECK PASSED: 1. Duplicate Requirements  -  The two requirements are distinct in wording and intent ("enter emergency shutdown" at 80°C vs "cooling fan shall turn on" at 85°C), so no textual or near-textual duplication is present.

CHECK PASSED: 2. Duplicate Block or Part Definitions  -  The model contains only one `part def CoolingSystem` and one `part def System`, with no parallel definitions of the same entity or duplicated interface/item definitions.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Redundant Relationships  -  No `satisfy`, `allocate`, `connect`, `bind`, or repeated specialization relationships are present in the provided fragment.

CHECK PASSED: 4. Redundant Behaviour Models  -  No state machines, action definitions, sequences, or duplicate message exchange models are present in the provided fragment.

CHECK PASSED: 5. Redundant Attributes & Constraints  -  The attributes `temperatureC`, `emergencyShutdownThresholdC`, and `coolingFanStartThresholdC` are each defined once only, with no repeated usage-level restatement or duplicated constraint application.

CHECK PASSED: 6. Redundant Decomposition  -  The decomposition is minimal (`System` contains one `CoolingSystem` part), but there is no evidence of redundant intermediate blocks, empty packages, or action pass-through layers in this fragment.

CHECK PASSED: 7. Unintentional vs. Intentional Redundancy  -  No duplicated structural or requirement elements were found, so there is no model redundancy requiring justification as intentional architectural redundancy.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-001]
Element(s):     thermal_system::EmergencyShutdown; thermal_system::CoolingFanActivation; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::emergencyShutdownThresholdC; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::coolingFanStartThresholdC
Check:          8. States that are never activated
Finding:        The model defines a cooling fan activation requirement at 85°C, while emergency shutdown is required at 80°C and the structural threshold for fan start is set to 86°C. If emergency shutdown prevents further normal operation once 80°C is reached, the fan activation condition is unreachable and therefore behaviourally redundant.
Evidence:       Requirement 1: "The system shall enter emergency shutdown when temperature reaches 80 degrees Celsius." Requirement 2: "The cooling fan shall turn on when temperature reaches 85 degrees Celsius." Attributes: `emergencyShutdownThresholdC : Real = 80.0;` and `coolingFanStartThresholdC : Real = 86.0;`
Score:          0.3  -  Significant defect because the model appears to include a redundant/unreachable cooling activation condition that could mislead design and verification.
Confidence:     MEDIUM  -  This depends on the inferred semantics that emergency shutdown precludes subsequent fan activation; the fragment contains no explicit behaviour/state model confirming that operational interpretation.
Risk:           Analysts may assume the cooling fan provides thermal mitigation in high-temperature conditions when, under the stated thresholds, shutdown occurs first and the fan logic may never execute. This can create inconsistent requirements, invalid verification expectations, and dead behaviour in implementation.
Recommendation: Remove or revise the redundant activation condition, or explicitly model the intended sequence. If cooling is supposed to act before shutdown, lower `CoolingFanActivation` and `coolingFanStartThresholdC` below the shutdown threshold and align the requirement and attribute values. If post-shutdown cooling is intentional, annotate that behaviour explicitly in the model.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| REDUNDANCY-001 | 0.3 | MEDIUM | Cooling fan activation appears unreachable because shutdown threshold occurs first and the structural fan threshold is even higher. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.3
Model Status: Critical deficiencies  -  major rework required before this model can be used
